concerns.text: The spec defines a circular validation loop for statistical significance. FR-003 and FR-010 mandate filtering data to 'storm intervals (Dst < -50 nT)' to compute correlations, then using those same Dst values to calculate the 'effective sample size' (Neff) for p-value adjustment. Since the selection of the dataset is entirely determined by the magnitude of the Dst variable, the resulting correlation coefficient and its significance test are tautological artifacts of the selection bias. The 'validation' (significance) is mathematically guaranteed by the predictor (Dst) used to define the sample, rendering the empirical finding non-trivial and scientifically unsound.

The methodology commits a 'conditioning on the outcome' error. By restricting the analysis exclusively to periods where Dst < -50 nT, the spec removes the variance in the dependent variable (geomagnetic activity) required to establish a meaningful correlation with independent variables (solar wind composition). Correlation coefficients computed on a truncated range of the dependent variable are biased and do not reflect the true relationship in the broader population. The claim that this validates 'predictive value' is invalid because the sample is pre-selected based on the outcome being predicted.

The application of the Pyper & Peterman (1998) effective sample size correction is methodologically mismatched for the proposed 'storm-only' analysis. This correction assumes a stationary time series with a known autocorrelation structure. However, storm intervals are non-stationary, transient events with rapidly changing dynamics. Applying a global lag-1 autocorrelation estimate derived from a truncated, non-stationary subset to adjust p-values violates the statistical assumptions of the test, leading to unreliable significance estimates.
